card.title: 5 Logical Fallacies You've Heard This Week
card.teaser: They hide in headlines, tweets, and family dinners. Once you know the names, you can't unsee them.
card.coverImagePrompt: A chessboard where the pieces are speech bubbles and question marks, locked in midgame tension, dramatic overhead lighting, editorial illustration style

lessons.title: The con artists of conversation
lessons.body: Someone wins an argument they had no business winning. The logic felt slippery — you couldn't quite catch it — and suddenly you're the one apologizing. What just happened? Probably a logical fallacy. Philosophers have been cataloging these rhetorical sleights of hand for 2,400 years. Learn five of the most common ones, and your B.S. detector becomes nearly unbluffable.

visual.imagePrompt: A magnifying glass hovering over a newspaper front page, revealing tiny hidden red arrows pointing to flawed logic, editorial illustration

lessons.title: #1 — The Straw Man
lessons.body: You say: 'We should invest more in public transit.' They reply: 'So you want to ban cars entirely?' That's a straw man — distorting your argument into a weaker, scarier version so it's easier to knock down. The trick works because it's emotionally vivid. The defense: calmly restate what you actually said, and ask them to address the real version.

keyTerms.definition: Misrepresenting an argument to make it easier to attack

visual.imagePrompt: A literal straw scarecrow being knocked over by a boxing glove, comic editorial illustration, whimsical but pointed

lessons.title: #2 — Ad Hominem
lessons.body: Instead of addressing the argument, attack the arguer. 'You can't talk about climate change — you flew here.' Maybe true. Maybe hypocritical. But it doesn't make the science wrong. Ad hominem turns the topic from 'is this claim correct?' into 'is this person likeable?' — and those are very different questions.

keyTerms.definition: Latin for 'to the person' — attacking the speaker instead of their argument

visual.imagePrompt: Two cartoon debaters: one pointing at a complex diagram, the other pointing rudely at the first person's face, stark color contrast

lessons.title: #3 — The Slippery Slope
lessons.body: 'If we allow A, then B, then C, then the collapse of civilization.' Each step sounds a little plausible, but the chain multiplies small uncertainties into catastrophe. Sometimes slopes really are slippery — but often they're invoked to scare you out of a reasonable first step. Ask for actual evidence that step A causes step B, not just rhetoric.

keyTerms.definition: Assuming one small step leads inevitably to extreme consequences without evidence

visual.imagePrompt: A cartoon figure tumbling down a steep slope of increasingly dire consequences, each labeled with an imagined disaster

lessons.title: #4 — Appeal to Authority (when misused)
lessons.body: 'A Nobel laureate said so, therefore it's true.' Expertise matters — a cardiologist's view on heart surgery counts. But authority abused means citing someone outside their field, or an isolated opinion against a scientific consensus. A famous physicist's take on nutrition is just... a take. The argument still has to stand on evidence, not the nameplate behind it.

keyTerms.definition: Using a source's status instead of actual evidence to prove a claim

visual.imagePrompt: A formal portrait of a credentialed expert in a laboratory, subtly out of their depth discussing an unrelated field

lessons.title: #5 — False Dichotomy (and why all of this matters)
lessons.body: 'You're either with us or against us.' Reality usually offers more than two options. False dichotomies hide the middle ground to force a choice. Once you learn these five — straw man, ad hominem, slippery slope, misused authority, and false dichotomy — you'll catch at least one in every news hour you consume. You're not more cynical. You're just harder to trick.

quiz.question: What is a 'straw man' fallacy?
options: Attacking the character of the speaker
Misrepresenting an argument to make it easier to attack
Assuming one step leads to catastrophe
quiz.explanation: You build a weaker, distorted version of the argument and attack that instead of the real one.

quiz.question: Which of these is a false dichotomy?
options: 'You either support this bill or you hate freedom.'
'If we allow this, everything will collapse.'
quiz.explanation: It presents only two extreme options when there are clearly more.

quiz.question: When is an appeal to authority actually legitimate?
options: Any time a famous person agrees
When the authority is a real expert in the relevant field and evidence supports them
quiz.explanation: Expert consensus in the right field, supported by evidence, is good reasoning. Name-dropping alone isn't.
